title: College Grads Return to School Amid Tough Job Market and Student Loan Caps
content:
After a demoralizing year searching for her first job out of college, Destiny Stone decided to return to school. A recent graduate with a degree in business administration, she had been working minimum-wage jobs while fruitlessly tailoring her resume. Now, she is pursuing an MBA financed through scholarships and federal loans. Stone's situation reflects a broader trend among newly minted college graduates facing a stagnant labor market. Like many during past economic downturns, they are seeking refuge in the classroom while waiting for hiring to improve.

However, this strategy is increasingly threatened by new caps on federal student loans. Unemployment among young US college graduates has tracked higher than the national average since the pandemic. Entry-level postings have plummeted across many industries, including tech and finance, which previously absorbed new talent readily. Furthermore, roles highly exposed to artificial intelligence have seen a significant decline in availability. As a result, this year's graduating seniors face a fierce competition, submitting dozens of applications for every available full-time position.

The combination of a weak job market and new borrowing limits creates a rare squeeze for recent graduates. For many students from low-income backgrounds, additional borrowing through private lenders is financially unrealistic. This forces some to abandon plans for graduate school and accept low-wage jobs instead. While experts note that advanced degrees can help protect against AI-driven job displacement, the new policies may limit access to these programs. Consequently, the ability to weather this economic storm by furthering one's education will increasingly depend on family wealth or risky private loans.
